title: Blood Projection
listing: Random decals based on surface normals
Material that changes color — aging animation
body:
To navigate the maze, the player can project their own blood. It is represented by projectiles that spawn a random decal from a reference list at the point of impact, while calculating the surface normal of the hit surface so the decal is oriented correctly.

That decal then changes color over time, simulating blood aging and making it possible to track how long the player has been moving through the maze.

title: NOTE — VFX
body: The modularity of the projection blueprint and its material would make it possible to adapt this system to other types of projectiles and surfaces, for a wide range of effects in other projects. It would also be possible to animate the decal shader to turn it into a visually rich but low-cost FX system.

title: C++ — Procedural Generation
listing: Grid generation — wall placement in C++
Dynamic placement of entrance and collectibles
body:
The maze generation was entirely built procedurally in C++. A grid is drawn according to the size defined in the properties, then walls are placed so there is always at least one opening.

The generation is designed so that no area can be inaccessible. Then the maze entrance and the collectibles are placed: regeneration potions, keys, spikes, and clues.

title: NOTE — C++
body: The challenge in this use of C++ was to bridge pure code and the blueprint. The maze blueprint properties are numerous enough to make the maze highly customizable while still keeping it functional.

title: Key UI/UX Design
body:
The challenge when building the key UI was matching each key pickup to its icon. Since every key had its own design, the idea was that collecting a key would trigger the correct icon in the interface, while still keeping the consistency of a single main key blueprint.

As a result, building a coherent key interface pushed the system further and made it reusable for other collectibles, which could also have many variants and their own inventory UI mapping.

title: NOTE — Interfaces
body:
The interfaces in this project were built with UMG Widgets. The key inventory interface was designed to be easy to modify and adaptable to other collectible types, with properties that define the icons and interactions associated with each collected item.

The stamina and health bars are simple sliders that could also be used for other resource types in other projects, with properties to adjust their appearance and behavior.
